data.title: Never-Trump Republicans will never be Democrats either
data.description:
George Conway’s decision to run for Congress as a Democrat in New York’s 12th District exposes a reality that Democrats have spent nearly a decade avoiding: Opposition to Donald Trump is not, by itself, a compelling political project. 

Don’t get me wrong — having worked on the digital team at Hillary for America and served as a presidential delegate for former Vice President Kamala Harris, I believe it is important to reject Trumpism with as broad a coalition as possible. 

But that effort is distinct from a coherent policy agenda that addresses the needs of voters. For too long, insiders have conflated the two — with arguably devastating consequences and electoral losses. Now, Conway’s odd congressional launch forces a necessary reckoning.

Conway is not running in a swing district or attempting to flip conservative voters or build a cross-partisan coalition. He is running in one of the safest Democratic seats in the country, in a Democratic primary, despite being a lifelong conservative Republican.

Conway’s anti-Trump credentials are well-earned. He has spent years denouncing Trump as corrupt, authoritarian and hostile to the rule of law. He co-founded the Lincoln Project, became a fixture in anti-Trump media and cast his break with the Republican Party as a moral stand in defense of democracy. 

What he has not done is repudiate the Republican project that produced Trump in the first place. He has also not fully articulated why he contemplated a senior role at Trump’s Department of Justice during his first term in office.

For most of his career, Conway supported and advanced conservative legal priorities: deregulation, hostility to labor protections, skepticism toward administrative governance, expansive executive power when wielded by Republicans and a judiciary designed to constrain redistribution and social policymaking. He emerged from the same Federalist Society world that built the courts through which Trump now governs. That record does not disappear just because Trump violated norms Conway cares about, too.

This is the central limitation of the Never-Trump Republicans — they are Republicans. Their critique of Trump is sincere, but partial by design. They oppose Trump not because he represents the culmination of Republican political economy, but because he violates professional norms and elite sensibilities.

Trump broke the rules. He embarrassed the party’s leadership class. He exercised power crudely rather than discreetly. Still, as recently as 2024, Conway tweeted that Roe v. Wade did “possibly irreparable” damage to “the court and American constitutionalism.”

None of this makes Conway insincere. His opposition to Trump is genuine. It is also deeply personal. Trump publicly mocked him and, in some sense, even divided his family. His antagonism is driven by a real belief in the most basic of democratic norms, but also by personal betrayal and grievance. Those motivations are understandable. They are also revealing. Conway’s politics is shaped by institutional outrage and personal rupture, not by a reckoning with the economic and legal distributions of power he once championed.

The problem is not that Democrats have welcomed Republicans who broke with Trump. That strategy helped defeat Trump in 2020, and Never-Trump Republican dissent continues to clarify that Trump’s presidency represents a genuine threat to constitutional governance. Figures such as Liz Cheney and Adam Kinzinger have played meaningful roles in exposing that danger.

The problem is that Democrats still increasingly treat Never-Trump Republicanism as a substitute for Democratic politics rather than a limited tactical alliance. We allow a spate of former Republican operatives with podcasts and viral twitter accounts to lecture us and define what is reasonable and what is “too woke,” to tell us where it all went wrong for progressives — when they themselves help steer us into this moment.

What began as necessity has hardened into habit, and it’s a bad one. Opposition to Trump has become a sufficient political identity, but it is an insufficient political platform. In the process, Democrats have outsourced moral authority to elite defectors while lowering expectations around material policy commitments and everyday political priorities.

Conway’s candidacy fits squarely within that pattern.

He is not running on a record that would continue the work and legacy of retiring Rep. Jerry Nadler (D-N.Y.), the iconic progressive leader he seeks to replace. In his 2 minute and 23-second launch video, Conway spends 3 seconds mentioning the “basic economic needs of all New Yorkers” and the rest of the video bashing Trump. 

Fellow Never-Trumper Miles Taylor, formerly known as the “anonymous New York Times op-ed writer” who took Trump on from the inside, replied to the video calling Conway’s candidacy a “no brainer” and writing “there is no one in this race who will stick it harder to Trump than George.”

Democrats face a straightforward choice. They can continue elevating elite former Republicans who have cashed in their distaste for Trump and their former Republican bonafides for celebrity and some sense of public forgiveness — or they can articulate and fight for a clear Democratic agenda of their own. It’s pretty clear that the former failed in 2024, while the latter succeeded in 2025. 

Kaivan Shroff was a 2024 Democratic National Convention presidential delegate and worked as a digital organizer on Hillary Clinton’s 2016 campaign. He is an attorney and senior advisor to the Institute for Education, a Washington nonprofit.

data.title: Trump makes case for US acquiring Greenland to Davos leaders
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday made his case for the U.S. to acquire Greenland before a swath of foreign leaders gathered at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

Trump joked that he was advised to leave the topic out of his speech, after European allies this week expressed reservations on his push to take over the Danish territory.

“Would you like me to say a few words on Greenland?” Trump asked the audience. “I was going to leave it out of the speech but I thought ‘I think I would have been reviewed very negatively.'” 

Trump noted that he has “tremendous respect for the people of Greenland and Denmark,” but “the fact is no nation or group of nations is in any position to be able to secure Greenland, other than the United States.”

He also suggested the U.S. would not use force to take over the island.

“We probably won’t get anything unless I decide to use excessive strength and force where we would be frankly unstoppable. But, I won’t do that,” Trump said at Davos. 

“I don’t have to use force. I don’t want to use force. I won’t use force. All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland,” he continued. 

The president went on to lay out the argument for the U.S. acquiring the Danish territory, saying it would only “strengthen” the NATO alliance. 

Trump also knocked Denmark, calling the country “ungrateful” in response to the U.S.’s help during World War II

“Without us, you’d all be speaking German and a little Japanese, perhaps,” the president said. 

Trump called for immediate negotiations for the U.S. to acquire Greenland, reiterating his stance that the island isn’t well enough protected.

data.title: Ty Cobb: 'Significant decline' in Trump mental faculties
data.description:
Former White House attorney Ty Cobb claimed President Trump is experiencing a “significant decline” in his mental faculties, pointing to the commander in chief’s Tuesday appearance at a White House press briefing to mark the one-year anniversary of his return to the Oval Office.

“I think there’s been a significant decline. He’s always been driven by narcissism. But I think the dementia and the cognitive decline are, you know, palpable, as do many experts, including many physicians,” Cobb told MS NOW’s Ari Melber on “The Beat,” in comments highlighted by Mediaite.

Dr. Jonathan Reiner, who served as a cardiologist for former Vice President Dick Cheney, on Monday pushed for a congressional inquiry into Trump’s fitness. Reiner noted a recent letter from Trump to Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Støre featuring the president seemingly connecting his threats to acquire Greenland with not getting the Nobel Peace Prize.

“This letter, and the fact that the president directed that it be distributed to other European countries, should trigger a bipartisan congressional inquiry into presidential fitness,” Reiner said on the social platform X.

At the beginning of the month, Trump said he was in “perfect health” and “aced” a cognitive exam.

“The White House Doctors have just reported that I am in ‘PERFECT HEALTH,’ and that I ‘ACED’ (Meaning, was correct on 100% of the questions asked!), for the third straight time, my Cognitive Examination, something which no other President, or previous Vice President, was willing to take,” Trump said on his Truth Social platform at the time.

Trump, 79, became the oldest president in American history when he was inaugurated again last January. Since he secured a second White House term, there has been a focus on his health and age, with some noting his hands that are often bruised.

Trump’s return to the presidency also follows former President Biden’s time in office, which at times was surrounded by questions about his age and health. Those concerns came to a head when he gave a rough debate performance against Trump in June 2024. He exited that year’s presidential campaign shortly after.

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: Listen live: Supreme Court weighs Trump's firing of Federal Reserve Gov. Lisa Cook
data.description:
The Supreme Court on Wednesday morning will weigh whether President Trump can legally oust Lisa Cook from the Federal Reserve Board of Governors over accusations of mortgage fraud. 

The oral arguments come as Trump has sought to reshape independent agencies that have long enjoyed protections against the president’s firing authority. Also looming over the case is the Justice Department’s criminal investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell, who is expected to attend the arguments, sources told The Hill’s sister network NewsNation.

The high court’s conservative majority has appeared sympathetic to Trump’s efforts to nix firing protections at other independent agencies, including the Federal Trade Commission and National Labor Relations Board. But the justices have suggested they view the nation’s central bank as possessing special status.

The event is scheduled to begin at 10 a.m. EST.

Listen above.

data.title: Watch live: Johnson, House GOP leaders give remarks as shutdown deadline looms
data.description:
Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) and GOP leaders will speak with reporters Wednesday morning as lawmakers face a partial government shutdown deadline.

Congress has so far only passed six of the 12 appropriations bills needed to fund the government beyond Jan. 30. Johnson is also faced with a dwindling majority ahead of the 2026 midterms as well as a growing culture of rebellion among the conference.

The press conference also come after Johnson became the first U.S. House Speaker to address the British Parliament. During his speech, he sought to reiterate the relationship between the two nations as the U.S. prepares to celebrate the 250th anniversary of independence.

The press conference is scheduled to begin at 10 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: How far is too far for Trump?
data.description:
When President Trump marched through Washington D.C.’s Lafayette Square at the height of the George Floyd protests in June 2020, he did so flanked by a phalanx of uniformed troops, including Gen. Mark Milley, then chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff.

Milley ultimately apologized for his role in Trump’s authoritarian photo-op, but that shocking moment now feels like foreshadowing amid Trump’s growing militarism in Minnesota, Venezuela and now Greenland.

Trump’s increasing aggression over Greenland can no longer be dismissed as mere rhetoric — at least, America’s European allies sure aren’t dismissing it. In one (presumably private) text message Trump posted to Truth Social on Monday, French President Emmanuel Macron voices his confusion. “I do not understand what you are doing on Greenland,” he remarks with concern. That’s a sentiment shared by nearly all the heads of Europe.

Trump is not only destroying European trust in America, as European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen suggested this week; he’s shattering what’s left of America’s fragile social contract with its own citizens. We’ve seen the true face of the MAGA movement, and it wears the same bloody jackboots of every authoritarian regime that came before. If this madness isn’t checked by the rule of law, we will soon find ourselves living in a nation where the only law that matters is, as Trump smugly told The New York Times, his “own morality.”

In a sign of just how brutal Trump’s governing vision has become, the White House on Saturday announced it would appeal a federal court ruling barring federal agents from arresting, tear-gassing and beating peaceful protestors in Minneapolis.

Think about that. What kind of people appeal a court order telling them they can’t brutalize and terrorize peaceful protesters? The kind of people for whom brutalizing and terrorizing their political opponents is now more important than even pretending to carry out a law enforcement mission. 

Despite Trump’s best efforts, millions of Americans are seeing the extreme examples of what happens when a radicalized ICE is given full impunity to violate the law. Nowhere was that clearer than the case of ChongLy Thao, the elderly U.S. citizen of Hmong descent whose image is now front-page news across the nation. Videos show masked ICE agents pulling a shirtless Thao from his home into the subzero Minnesota winter after raiding his home at gunpoint.  

Federal agents never produced a warrant, and didn’t even seem to know who they should be arresting. Thao offered to show his ID. Agents refused to look. Hours later ICE realized they’d made a mistake — that Thao had no criminal record and was an American citizen, and that they had raided a home without a warrant. This was the kind of state terrorism Thao had fled Laos to avoid. ICE agents eventually dropped Thao back at his home, leaving him to walk through his shattered front door without so much as an apology.  

According to Vice President JD Vance, those agents enjoy the same “absolute immunity” as every other ICE agent, regardless of whether they broke the law or not. No wonder most Americans now believe that ICE is a bigger danger to their communities than the undocumented immigrants they are allegedly there to deport.  

Things are no better internationally, where an unhinged Trump is threatening to impose Europe-wide tariffs until Denmark hands over Greenland. Trump’s threat of 10 percent tariffs against a host of European nations sent the S&P 500 index tumbling  by roughly $750 billion on Tuesday — almost exactly Greenland’s estimated value. Trump’s saber-rattling has already cost the U.S. more than it would gain from owning the island outright. 

In one particularly bizarre rant, Trump claimed he wanted Greenland as a consolation prize after being denied the Nobel Peace Prize. “I no longer feel an obligation to think purely of Peace,” Trump said.

Denied his golden bauble, Trump now feels no concern about plunging the world into economic turmoil and possible war. Are we really going to pretend these are the thoughts of a mentally healthy leader? A man willing to threaten war with his allies over a peace prize has no business running anything, let alone the most powerful nation in the world.

As Europe begins to look toward a post-American regional alliance, Trump — and, by extension, the U.S. — will find themselves politically marginalized and economically blackballed. It will be regular Americans who pay the price for Trump’s growing madness, not the MAGA elites who are steering our country into authoritarian calamity. Left unchecked, the results of Trump’s militarism will be devastating for our national future.  

Our elected leaders are running out of time to stop America’s plunge into darkness. 

Max Burns is a veteran Democratic strategist and founder of Third Degree Strategies.

data.title: Trump: Insurrection Act 'does make life a lot easier'
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday said invoking the Insurrection Act to quell protests in Minnesota would “make life a lot easier,” even as he dismissed the prospect as unnecessary for the time being.

In a sit-down interview with NewsNation’s “Katy Pavlich Tonight,” the president did not rule out the possibility of invoking the law in the future.

“I don’t think it is yet. It might be at some point,” Trump said, when asked whether he thinks it might be necessary.

He noted the act is invoked more often than people realize, saying it’s “actually very common” and “something I would have no problem doing if I think we needed it.”

“Over 40 percent of the presidents used it. Some presidents used it, actually, many times, and it does make life a lot easier,” the president continued. “You don’t go through the court system, you don’t — you know, it’s just a much easier thing to do.”

“But I don’t think we need it at this point, and hopefully we won’t need it,” he added. “But I would not have any problem with invoking it if we needed it.”

Trump last week threatened to invoke the 1807 Insurrection Act, hours after an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer shot and injured a Venezuelan migrant during a traffic stop, when the target fled on foot and the officer was attacked with a shovel.

The incident — which followed the fatal shooting of a Minneapolis woman by an ICE officer the week before — came as tensions were already high in the city amid a wave of protests against the Trump administration’s immigration policies.

“If the corrupt politicians of Minnesota don’t obey the law and stop the professional agitators and insurrectionists from attacking the Patriots of I.C.E., who are only trying to do their job, I will institute the INSURRECTION ACT, which many Presidents have done before me, and quickly put an end to the travesty that is taking place in that once great State,” Trump wrote in a post on Truth Social last Thursday.

He said Friday, however, that there is no reason for him to invoke the Insurrection Act yet, telling reporters, “I don’t think there’s any reason right now to use it, but if I needed it, I’d use it.” 

The Insurrection Act allows the president to send federal troops to an area in an effort to tamp down civil unrest. The last time it was invoked was the early 1990s.

data.title: Watch live: House Judiciary panel convenes hearing on Minnesota fraud
data.description:
The House Judiciary Subcommittee on Crime and Federal Government Surveillance on Wednesday is looking into the alleged abuse of federal funds in Minnesota.

Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) has come under heavy criticism from the GOP over failing to halt fraud that may have cost the state billions of dollars. In response, the Trump administration froze federal funding to child care providers in the state and deployed federal agents to Minneapolis — prompting legal challenges.

The hearing also comes after the House Oversight and Energy and Environment committees launched probes into the fraud allegations.

Wednesday’s event is scheduled to begin at 10 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: What to watch as Lisa Cook heads to Supreme Court
data.description:
Close

Thank you for signing up!

Subscribe to more newsletters here

The latest in politics and policy. Direct to your inbox. Sign up for the The Gavel newsletter

President Trump’s power over the Federal Reserve goes before the Supreme Court Wednesday in a blockbuster review of the central bank governors’ firing protections. 

As the president wages war against the Fed for not lowering interest rates quicker, Trump has looked to sack Fed governor Lisa Cook by accusing her of mortgage fraud, which she denies. 

Lower courts sided with Cook. The justices have punted a decision for months on Trump’s emergency appeal until they hold oral arguments, which are set to kick off at 10 a.m. EST. 

Here’s what to watch for as the argument gets underway: 

How the justices interpret ‘for cause’ 

The Federal Reserve Act of 1913 only permits the president to remove Fed governors “for cause.” 

The statute does not explicitly define “cause.” Wednesday’s case turns on how the justices interpret it. 

Everyone concedes that disagreeing with a governor’s votes on interest rates isn’t sufficient. Beyond that, the sides diverge. 

The Trump administration contends that “for cause” gives the president broad discretion to fire Fed governors for reasons related to their conduct, ability, fitness or competence. 

If the justices agree, the administration says it’s an easy case and that the mortgage fraud accusations against Cook easily pass the test. 

Cook’s lawyers push back. They say Congress intended “for cause” to be comparable to the standard in place at a handful of other independent agencies, which spell out inefficiency, neglect or malfeasance as the three preconditions to firing. 

Watch to see if the justices agree with the Trump administration’s proposal, or if they agree with the concerns of Cook that it would imperil the Fed’s independence. 

Does Cook’s due process argument gain steam 

Regardless of whether Trump had valid “cause,” Cook will advance several other arguments Wednesday. 

She asserts that due process protections under the Constitution and federal law guaranteed her a hearing to deny the mortgage fraud accusations.  

“She unequivocally does, and she is prepared to refute the allegations in an appropriate forum,” Cook’s lawyers wrote. 

The Trump administration says no such hearing is legally required and that Cook has not explained what it would look like or how it would make any difference. 

Additionally, the administration will try to convince the justices that even if she was wrongfully removed, the lower courts had no power to grant an injunction reinstating Cook as the litigation plays out. 

If the justices tip their hand on FTC decision 

The case comes to the court weeks after the justices heard arguments on Trump’s power to fire Rebecca Slaughter, a member of the Federal Trade Commission (FTC). 

The cases both involve the bounds of Trump’s presidential power, but they raise distinct issues. 

At the Fed, Trump purports to have valid cause in sacking Cook.  

But at the FTC, the president does not. Instead, he argues Slaughter’s firing protections infringe on the separation of powers — a broad assertion of presidential power that appears to have drawn sympathy with the Supreme Court’s conservative majority. 

The Trump administration has not made that argument for the Fed, which the justices have signaled has special status that may justify its protections. 

Under the court’s typical procedures, the justices would’ve taken an initial vote on Slaughter’s case in the days following the December argument. The opinion would’ve been assigned, and drafting would commence. 

Watch on Wednesday to see if the justices’ comments tip their hand on what they plan to do in Slaughter’s case. 

How Powell’s attendance impacts the case 

Wednesday’s argument only directly concerns Cook’s firing, but watch to see if the justices connect it to Trump’s broader threats against the central bank for not lowering interest rates quicker. 

Fed Chair Jerome Powell will attend Wednesday’s argument at the Supreme Court, a source familiar with his plans tells our colleague Sylvan Lane, The Hill’s business editor. 

For months, Trump has mused about firing Powell. But the president has yet to do so as the clock ticks closer to May, when Powell’s term as chair ends (he may remain on the board until 2028).  

In the lead-up to Wednesday’s argument, things took a turn. The Justice Department issued a subpoena to the Fed, bringing its criminal investigation into Powell to light. 

The investigation centers on congressional testimony Powell gave concerning the Fed’s multi-billion-dollar renovation of its headquarters, which has faced significant cost overruns. Powell denies wrongdoing and has cast the investigation as an attempt to influence the central bank’s monetary policy. 

As the Fed’s chair, Powell is formally named as a defendant in Cook’s lawsuit. 

His attendance makes him the elephant in the room. Government officials typically sit near the front of the public gallery, which is in the back half of the courtroom behind the bar section.  

We’ll be in the room Wednesday to watch the justices — and Powell. So stay tuned. 

Welcome to The Gavel, The Hill’s weekly courts newsletter from Ella Lee and Zach Schonfeld. Reach out to us on X (@ByEllaLee, @ZachASchonfeld) or Signal (elee.03, zachschonfeld.48). Sign up here or in the box below:   

IN FOCUS

Looking back at 1 year of Trump v. the courts 

President Trump entered the second year of his White House term this week. 

As his first year rapidly remade Washington, it flooded courts across the country. 

Democratic state attorneys general, legal groups formed to oppose Trump, civil rights organizations and more asked judges hundreds of times to to rule pieces of Trump’s agenda unlawful — and halt them in the meantime. 

Lawsuits began flying while Trump was still in the Capitol Rotunda for his inauguration ceremony. 

Within minutes of Chief Justice John Roberts administering the oath, three lawsuits sought to dismantle the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) before it even got started.  

They claimed DOGE was a federal advisory committee that wasn’t following transparency requirements. No judge has yet to endorse the argument, and some of the cases were later dropped. 

Still, it became the first of hundreds of legal battlefronts to come.  

And in the early months of the administration, challenges to DOGE dominated. 

Some contested the administration’s assertion that Elon Musk was not formally in charge of the group. Others sought to subject DOGE to the Freedom of Information Act. But by and large, the cases concerned efforts to implant DOGE personnel at agencies across the government and allegations they were violating the Privacy Act. 

Government unions led the charge in many of the cases. And as they would go on to do on issue after issue, left-leaning legal groups like Democracy Forward and Democracy Defenders Fund represented many of the plaintiffs in court. 

And within months, they secured dozens of injunctions from federal district judges, stymying Trump’s agenda. Temporarily. 

Repeatedly over the past year, the administration filed emergency appeals all the way to the Supreme Court, almost always emerging successful. 

In total, the administration brought 32 Supreme Court emergency appeals. That’s more than former President Biden’s Justice Department filed during his entire four years in office. 

Trump’s critics say the barrage reflects how he is acting lawlessly. 

California Attorney General Rob Bonta (D), speaking Tuesday at an event celebrating the 54 lawsuits his office has brought against Trump, summed up the president’s first year as a “sweeping, often illegal campaign to remake America.” 

“We said then if he breaks the law, we will take him to court. We are here today to say we have fulfilled that promise,” Bonta said. 

The president’s legal allies push back, suggesting the nonstop pace reveals how lower judges have overstepped their authority by granting one injunction after another.  

The real score is the administration’s record at the high court, those allies argue. 

The exact tally depends on how you count a few mixed rulings, but on our scorecard, the administration has lost only four cases and emerged victorious in roughly 20. 

Among others, that list includes greenlighting the administration’s efforts to fire more than 16,000 probationary employees, ban transgender troops from serving openly in the military, revoke temporary legal protections extended to more than 300,000 Venezuelans and parole for 532,000 people from Latin American countries, freeze $65 million in teacher preparation grants and lay off roughly half the Education Department 

Only in a few cases, like Trump’s bid to deploy the National Guard in the Chicago area, did the court rule against the president. 

What’s in store for his second year? Here are five issues that will define the legal landscape: 

  1. Does Trump’s string of Supreme Court victories continue as the justices begin handing down final decisions on the merits of Trump’s policies — like his sweeping tariffs and efforts to control independent agencies — rather than preliminary decisions on the emergency docket? 
  1. Will the Justice Department criminally indict more prominent Trump foes, like Powell, Sen. Adam Schiff (D-Calif.), Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.) and former Special Counsel Jack Smith? 
  1. Will the Supreme Court rein in judges who’ve granted nationwide relief to plaintiffs by signing off on class actions in the wake of the justices clawing back universal injunctions? 
  1. Will Trump’s rapid pace of executive orders, which now exceeds 200, slow down? 
  1. Will Trump invoke the Insurrection Act? 

Alien Enemies Act back in court 

One of the most prominent legal battles of Trump’s first year back in the White House returns to the courtroom later this week. 

Last March, Trump invoked the Alien Enemies Act of 1798 to swiftly deport alleged Venezuelan gang members. 

It began with sending dozens of migrants to a Salvadoran megaprison. Another wave in April was underway when the Supreme Court intervened in the middle of the night to halt the flights at the pleas of the American Civil Liberties Union. 

The deportations became a prominent sign last spring of Trump’s aggressive immigration agenda.  

In the months since, the legal battle has calmed in some respects. It’s about to return to the spotlight. 

On Thursday, all 17 active judges on the 5th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals will convene at the federal courthouse in New Orleans to hear oral arguments on whether Trump legally invoked the Alien Enemies Act. 

It’s slated to be a blockbuster session that appears likely destined for the Supreme Court. More than a half-dozen other legal challenges to Trump’s use of the law are on hold, all awaiting word from the 5th Circuit. 

The Alien Enemies Act allows the president to summarily deport migrants when a foreign nation perpetrates an “invasion” or predatory “incursion.” It has only been invoked three prior times: the War of 1812 and World Wars I and II. 

Trump claims Tren de Aragua is perpetrating an invasion against the U.S., and he has tied the transnational criminal gang to the Venezuelan government. 

In April, the Supreme Court ruled that migrants detained under the Alien Enemies Act must receive notice and an opportunity to challenge their removal before they are deported. 

That decision leaves several questions unanswered that have been simmering ever since. The 5th Circuit will get the next bite at the apple Thursday. 

American Civil Liberties Union lawyer Lee Gelernt will argue the Alien Enemies Act requires a a declaration of war or a military attack for the president to invoke the law.  

The statute requires the attack be committed by a foreign nation, and Gelernt will make the case that Trump can’t tie his concerns about Tren de Aragua to deposed President Nicolás Maduro’s regime. 

And even if the president did lawfully invoke the statute, Gelernt will argue that migrants should be afforded at least 30-days’ notice to ensure the Trump administration can’t deport them without judicial review. 

The Trump administration will be represented by Drew Ensign, who leads immigration litigation for the Justice Department. He will assert that the Alien Enemies Act compels the courts to provide the president extreme deference.  

Tren de Aragua has perpetrated an invasion “by any metric” and is sufficiently connected to Maduro’s government, administration wrote in court filings. The administration has also argued requiring 30-days’ notice is unwarranted.  

SIDEBAR

5 top docket updates    

  1. No tariffs: The Supreme Court released two more batches of opinions this past week, but still no decision in the highly anticipated battle over Trump’s tariffs. The next opinion release day remains unclear, but the justices are not currently scheduled to take the bench again until after President’s Day. 
  1. California map upheld: A divided three-judge panel upheld California’s new congressional map set to add several Democratic-leaning seats as part of the mid-decade redistricting war. The California GOP has already provided notice it is appealing directly to the Supreme Court. 
  1. Offshore wind projects resume: Separate judges issued orders allowing construction to resume on the Empire Wind and Coastal Virginia offshore wind projects 
  1. SCOTUS says candidates can challenge mail ballot deadline: The Supreme Court ruled Rep. Mike Bost (R-Ill.) has legal standing to challenge Illinois’ practice of accepting mail ballots that arrive after Election Day. The justices will review the legality of the practice later this term. 
  1. College basketball indictment: The Justice Department unsealed an indictment against 26 people over an alleged point-shaving scheme to fix college basketball games. It marks the latest federal charges against athletes over betting accusations. 

In other news    

  • Job posting — but is there an opening?: The chief judge in the Eastern District of Virginia has begun soliciting applications to become the district’s interim U.S. attorney, the post that the Trump administration claims Lindsey Halligan occupies. It’s the latest twist in the battle over Halligan’s disqualification.  judge disqualified her. 
  • Billboard top artist: Leonard Jacoby, a cofounder of the law firm that helped transform mass legal advertising and make the legal system more available to everyday Americans, such as the use of billboards, died at 83, the Los Angeles Times reported.  
  • Kiefer Sutherland arrested: “Designated Survivor” star Keifer Sutherland was arrested in Hollywood over allegations he assaulted and threatened a ride-share driver, according to NBC News. 

ORDER LIST

Cases the Supreme Court is taking up — or passing on — this term.  

IN: Roundup weed killer, geofence warrants 

The court took up four new cases at Friday’s conference, perhaps the final cases that will be argued this term. 

First, the court agreed to hear Bayer’s appeal to limit liability for its Roundup weed killer, which has come under thousands of lawsuits alleging it causes cancer and did not include a proper warning label.  

Bayer argues the state lawsuits are preempted by federal law, which does not require the warning. The case is Monsanto Company v. Durnell. 

In Chatrie v. United States, the Supreme Court took up its next major Fourth Amendment case. 

The justices will review whether geofence warrants, which allow law enforcement to seize information on cellphones online at a location during a specific time window, violate constitutional protections against unreasonable searches. 

The court will also review an employment retirement plan case interpreting the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974, (ERISA).  

In Anderson v. Intel Corporation Investment Policy Committee, the justices will examine whether plaintiffs alleging their employer’s plan underperformed must show a “meaningful benchmark,” which would make it more difficult to bring ERISA lawsuits. 

Finally, the court agreed to hear Hikma Pharmaceuticals USA v. Amarin Pharma. 

The fight between the two pharma companies poses broad implications for so-called “skinny labels,” which allow generic drugmakers to bring products to market faster by carving out a product’s brand-name, patented use on the label. 

OUT: SCOTUS fee waivers 

Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson, the court’s juniormost liberal justice, has thoughts on how the court handles filings from incarcerated defendants. 

The Supreme Court chooses what cases it hears. But anyone can try their luck, so long as they submit a $300 filing fee and pay to print 40 bound copies (yes, this is still a requirement in 2026, and Jackson noted it’s usually the much higher cost). 

The justices routinely waive the fee for poor incarcerated defendants who can’t afford it. But if the defendant then repeatedly tries to file frivolous litigation, the court cuts them off. 

The court did so Tuesday for a handful of defendants, including Danny Howell, who was sentenced to 70 years in prison after being convicted of child molestation and other charges. The clerk will no longer accept “any further petitions in noncriminal matters” from Howell unless he pays the fee.  

“Even if Howell were to identify meritorious grounds for habeas relief or wanted to bring a justifiable challenge to his conditions of confinement, he will now be prevented from doing so unless he pays the filing fee—no matter what,” Jackson wrote in dissent. 

“A categorical, forward-looking filing bar is a questionable restriction as to any litigant who cannot afford to pay a filing fee. For me, it is an intolerable one as to incarcerated individuals.” 

ON THE DOCKET

Don’t be surprised if additional hearings are scheduled throughout the week. But here’s what we’re watching for now:  

Today: 

  • The Supreme Court will hear oral arguments over Trump’s efforts to fire Federal Reserve board of governors member Lisa Cook. The case is Trump v. Cook.  

Thursday: 

  • Former special counsel Jack Smith will testify publicly before the House Judiciary Committee. 
  • The entire U.S. Court of Appeals for the 5th Circuit is set to hear oral arguments in a challenge two Venezuelan nationals, represented by the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU), brought to Trump’s invocation of the Alien Enemies Act. 
  • The U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit is set to hear oral arguments in transgender service members’ and prospective service members’ challenge to Trump’s order banning transgender troops from serving in the military. 
  • A federal judge in Washington state is set to hold a summary judgment hearing in 16 Democratic state attorneys general’s challenge to the Education Department’s discontinuation of mental health funding under the Mental Health Service Professional Demonstration Grant Program (MHSP) for low-income/rural schools and Mental Health Services Grant Program (SBMH). 
  • A federal judge in Washington, D.C., is set to hold a preliminary injunction hearing over Trump’s bid to renovate the White House ballroom.  

Friday: 

  • A federal judge in New York is set to hold a preliminary injunction hearing in five states’ challenge to the freezing of $10 billion in federal funds by the Administration for Children and Families (ACF). 
  • A federal judge in New York has ordered an Altoona Police Department officer to testify about the department’s arrest procedures as the judge mulls Luigi Mangione’s request to suppress key evidence at his criminal trial. 

Monday: 

  • The Supreme Court will announce orders.  

Tuesday: 

  • The Senate Judiciary Subcommittee on the Constitution will hold a hearing on Trump’s efforts to restrict birthright citizenship for children born to parents in the country unlawfully. 

WHAT WE’RE READING

  1. The Wall Street Journal’s Michelle Hackman, Kris Maher and Brenna T. Smith: The Standoff That Has Turned Minnesota Into a Tinderbox 
  1. The National Law Journal’s Jimmy Hoover: “’The Technology Is There’: Supreme Court Practitioners Quietly Embracing AI” 
  1. CourtWatch: The Rabbit Hole: ‘Metastasizing Evil’ 
  1. Politico E&E News’s Niina H. Farah: Legal tests await Trump’s offshore energy agenda in 2026 
  1. ABC News’s Matt Rivers, Janice McDonald, and Armando Garcia: Lawyers allege Dept. of Homeland Security is denying legal counsel to Minnesota detainees

data.title: Trump must mend, not widen, the rift between MAGA and pro-lifers
data.description:
For most of my adult life, I wasn’t very pro-life. I never thought abortion was a great thing, especially when used as birth control for irresponsible people, but I was always more interested in other issues.

And, if I’m being brutally honest — though it was always an abstract fear — there was that dread in the back of every young man’s head that someone they’re dating would become pregnant and, well, you wouldn’t necessarily want to deny that woman her “rights,” would you?

I never wanted kids when I was younger. I can’t imagine any woman wanting to have a child with my younger self, either. But eventually, everyone grows up and their opinions change. Now, I’m married, and I have two great kids. I went from living in fear of children as a concept and never wanting any to wishing I could have a dozen.

That brings me to the annual March for Life. What used to be a commemoration of the Roe v Wade decision is now a celebration of its demise and the continued fight to protect life across the country. It’s coming up on January 23.

As that date approaches, it is important to note that there is a growing divide between the pro-life movement and President Trump’s MAGA movement. Whether that gap will continue to grow or whether Trump will close it remains to be seen. But with the midterms looming, this problem cannot be ignored.

Wherever you fall on the issue of protecting unborn human life, the pro-life coalition will be a crucial part of any coalition making it possible for Republicans to maintain control of Congress after this year’s election. 

The Daily Wire recently reported that a “coalition of pro-life leaders urged the Trump administration on Tuesday to take action against ‘attacks on the unborn and most vulnerable’ they said were coming from inside the White House.” Their issue was that the Food and Drug Administration had “approved a generic form of the abortion pill in October and maintained the mail-order abortion regulations.”

This move caused Sen. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.) to say he had “lost confidence in the leadership at the FDA.”

FDA Commissioner Marty Makary has even pushed off past the midterms publishing any safety reviews of the abortion pill. If you wanted to actively antagonize pro-life voters, this is what you would do.

Add to this the fact that one of Makary’s most-trusted deputies, Vinay Prasad, has publicly declared himself “a Bernie Sanders liberal” and “pro universal health care. Pro wealth tax. Pro choice. Etc.” It only adds to the rift.

Then there’s the fact that Trump recently called for Republicans to end Obamacare subsidies by giving the money directly to the American people — but “you have to be a little flexible on Hyde, you know that. You gotta be a little flexible, you gotta work something.” The Hyde Amendment is the appropriations provision that prevents federal money from being used to fund abortion. Trump’s call for “flexibility” did not and does not sit well with pro-life groups.

Activist Lila Rose has posted to her X account, “Trump calls to make the Hyde Amendment ‘flexible,’ potentially opening the door to taxpayer-funded abortion. The GOP must stand firm for human life. No exceptions. No inch given. If you sacrifice Hyde, you sacrifice innocent human children.”

For the pro-life coalition, overturning Roe was not a finish line — it was a checkpoint. It is a victory to be celebrated, but the fight continues. It is doubtful that the movement’s activists thought that fight would come at them from leadership at the FDA or the White House.

For Republicans to win in November, the entire coalition will need to pull together to overcome Democrats. To alienate one of the most motivated and grateful wings of that coalition is not smart politics. It will make the fight unnecessarily more difficult.

The people for whom abortion is a priority that moves their vote will always vote against Republicans, as they are pot-committed to the concept and will not waver. Voters for whom life is the issue that moves their vote, on the other hand, will not vote for someone wavering on abortion — either in a clinic or by pill. The White House needs to be unambiguous on the issue, lest it lose a crucial voting bloc in a year where every vote will matter.

Derek Hunter is host of The Derek Hunter Show, weekdays from 3 p.m. to 6 p.m. on WMAL in Washington. He is a former staffer for the late Sen. Conrad Burns (R-Mont.).
